data.title: Henry McMaster endorses Pamela Evette to succeed him as South Carolina governor
data.description:
South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster (R) on Thursday endorsed Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette (R) to succeed him when he leaves office at the end of the year, emphasizing their partnership.

“Together, we broke records, creating new jobs. We delivered the largest tax cut in state history by a lot. We won at the Supreme Court, protecting the lives of the born and unborn. We closed that revolving door that had let violent criminals out, over and over,” McMaster said, praising his work with Evette in a video shared by her campaign.

“And when it came to President Trump – Pamela Evette stood with me from day one – eight years ago – helping me bring the America First agenda home to South Carolina. Now, I’m proud to endorse Pamela Evette to be our next governor.”

Republican Reps. Nancy Mace and Ralph Norman are also in the running for the open governor’s mansion in the red state, as is state Attorney General Alan Wilson and state Sen. Josh Kimbrell. Polling hasn’t signaled a clear frontrunner. 

Whoever emerges from the June primary is expected to sail through the November general election. Cook Political Report rates it a “solid Republican” seat, and President Trump won by double digits in 2024. 

Trump has yet to weigh in on the race. The president backed Mace’s last congressional campaign and previously supported both Norman and Wilson, but also has a long relationship with McMaster, whose endorsement could help clear the path for Trump to get involved in the battle. 

Mace in a statement called Evette “a nice lady” but shrugged off the endorsement. 

“But endorsements don’t fix potholes. Endorsements don’t eliminate the state income tax. And endorsements don’t put billions into roads and bridges. Results do. And my record does too. South Carolina families aren’t looking for more of the same. They’re looking for a strong leader with a plan, and we’re the only campaign that has one,” Mace said.

data.title: O'Reilly: Bondi decided to 'throw some hand grenades' in hearing
data.description:
Political commentator Bill O’Reilly said Wednesday that Attorney General Pam Bondi decided to “throw some hand grenades” in combative testimony before a House panel examining the Justice Department, including its handling of the Jeffrey Epstein investigation.

“It’s obvious the Democratic Party wants to link Donald Trump to some kind of crime involving Epstein,” O’Reilly said during an appearance on NewsNation’s “CUOMO.”

“They’re never going to stop doing that and Bondi knows it, so what she decided to do was go in and throw some hand grenades and just blow the whole thing up and bring attention the charade that it really is,” he added.

Bondi sparred with lawmakers as she sat for her first appearance before the House Judiciary Committee since taking office last year.

The routine hearing quickly devolved into a series of heated exchanges, with shouting and personal insults being hurled between the country’s top federal prosecutor and Democrats on the panel.

She also clashed with Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), a co-sponsor of the Epstein Files Transparency Act, after he said the DOJ only unredacted the email of someone “likely incriminated” in the documents because they were caught “red-handed.”

Bondi later called Massie a “failed politician” who had “Trump derangement syndrome.”

Another testy exchange involved the committee’s ranking member, Jamie Raskin (D-Md.), a former lawyer and constitutional law professor, in which Bondi said he was a “washed-up loser lawyer.”

At least 11 of Epstein’s victims were in the audience and were asked at one point by Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.) to raise their hands if they had yet to meet with the DOJ during Trump’s term to which each of them did.  

“My question would have been to them, ‘well you have the court to sue civilly. There are thousands of lawyers who would take your case free. Why don’t you pursue that end and then in depositions, if there’s something that is shocking or true or you can prove it, then you take it to the Justice Department,” O’Reilly said Wednesday.

Host Jesse Weber pushed back on whether the former Fox News host thought the administration was making good on its campaign promise to investigate Epstein’s crimes.

“I just wonder, is this backfiring?” Weber asked.

“It does not seem like this whole thing is being run responsibly,” O’Reilly replied.

“It’s a stunt to get Trump, and all Trump supporters know that,” the commentator continued. “Now, whether the independents or people who don’t follow the news, I don’t know what their frame of reference is.”

Recent polling has found that Americans are generally unsatisfied with the federal government’s handling of the files tied to the disgraced financier, with many believing information has been intentionally withheld.

A CNN survey conducted just over a week before the latest batch of Epstein files was released found that just 6 percent of Americans were satisfied with what the DOJ has unveiled so far in the case, including 12 percent of Republicans and 3 percent of Democrats and independents.

data.title: This amendment can protect our elections from foreign interference
data.description:
In 2026, the money in politics will be flowing. This year’s midterms are expected to set a new advertising record — an estimated $11 billion. How much of that money will come from foreign sources? Unfortunately, we don’t know the answer, but “dark money” is sure to have an outsized impact.

According to recent estimates, the major political nonprofit groups tied to the 2024 presidential candidates raised almost $900 million from donors whose identities remained entirely secret. The largest single donation, at $97.5 million, came to Forward Future USA Action from an “unidentified person or group.” 

You read that right: More than a year removed from Election 2024, we still don’t even know who made the single largest dark-money donation in the presidential race. Was it a foreign billionaire? Or a foreign government? Why was the donation made in the first place? 

As it stands now, non-citizens can influence U.S. elections and domestic policymaking through undisclosed donations to super PACs, ballot initiatives and other loopholes. Foreign billionaires with unknown or ulterior motives can funnel massive funds into the electoral process, swaying federal, state and local politics. 

As the 2026 midterms loom, there is no greater priority for U.S. policymakers than to ensure our elections are safe, secure and funded exclusively by Americans — not foreign interests. Our electoral system is the backbone of American democracy, and it is our duty to protect that system from undue foreign influence. 

With each passing week in Washington, D.C., it seems like a new dark money group forms to win hearts and minds, issue by issue. This opens countless doors to foreign influence, including interference from adversaries like China, Iran or Russia. Across the pond, the United Kingdom is investigating foreign political interference after former Reform Party lawmaker Nathan Gill was convicted of accepting bribes from Russia. China, meanwhile, continues to exert influence in Myanmar and other countries.

These are warning signs for us stateside. 

To properly heed such warnings, Congress can take action by passing the For Our Freedom Amendment, which would empower Washington, D.C., and the states to address out-of-control dark money in our campaigns and elections. This constitutional amendment would restore the ability of lawmakers to decide whether and how to regulate political spending, without mandating specific reforms or advancing the agenda of a specific party.

Without a constitutional amendment, even common-sense efforts will continue to be blocked by the U.S. judicial system, while state laws run the risk of being undone. In July 2025, for instance, the U.S. Court of Appeals in Boston struck down a Maine law that aimed to block foreign governments and foreign-owned corporations from spending to influence Maine ballot initiatives. Following Supreme Court precedent, the appellate court ruled that such restrictions violate the First Amendment.

In other words, today’s lawmakers don’t have the constitutional authority to ban foreign money in our elections. The For Our Freedom Amendment would change that, restoring power to the people by granting our elected officials the power to choose whether to pass laws that limit the influence of big money in politics; enacting strong transparency requirements; and protecting the integrity of U.S. elections from bad foreign actors. 

Most Americans support campaign finance reform, including limitations on political spending. Democrat or Republican, most Americans recognize that foreign interference in U.S. elections is unacceptable. Yet we live in a world where undue foreign influence is more likely than ever before — from social media to online fundraising and artificial intelligence. The omnipresence of AI bots and other potential weapons of foreign governments makes reform all the more necessary, given that chatbots have been shown to meaningfully shift political opinions. 

Unless we keep America’s elections free from foreign interference, we won’t really live in a free America with a bright future. That future must be ours, and ours alone. Will Congress finally take action?  

Jeff Clements is the co-founder and CEO of the nonprofit American Promise. Angela Paxton is a Republican state senator in Texas who represents District 8.

data.title: Dozens of Democrats call for removal of newly appointed OB-GYNs from vaccine advisory panel
data.description:
Dozens of Democratic lawmakers are calling for the removal of two OB-GYNs who were recently appointed to the nation’s top vaccine advisory committee, citing the doctors’ “well-documented history” of anti-vaccine ideology.

The lawmakers said in a Thursday letter to Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. that they were concerned about the Jan. 13 appointments of Dr. Adam Urato and Dr. Kimberly Biss to the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

Urato is an OB-GYN specializing in maternal-fetal medicine, and Biss is an OB-GYN from St. Petersburg, Fla.

The letter cited comments made by Biss, a self-described “anti-vaxxer,” during a congressional hearing in 2023 in which she claimed the COVID-19 vaccine increased the risk of miscarriages in pregnant women. Studies have found no scientific link between the two.

The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) continues to recommend that pregnant women be vaccinated against COVID-19 and receive boosters, asserting it is safe and there is no evidence of adverse maternal or fetal effects from using it.

Evidence has shown, however, that severe COVID-19 infections can have negative impacts on pregnancy, ranging from an increased risk of complications in patients with underlying health conditions to preterm delivery.  

The lawmakers noted that the U.S. has the highest maternal mortality rate of any developed nation, a trend that was exacerbated by the pandemic. Maternal mortality reached a peak of 33.8 deaths per 100,000 live births in February 2022, according to CDC data.

“All of our public health professionals should be working to decrease these harrowing statistics,” they wrote to Kennedy.

“Yet, the Trump administration and the Department of Health and Human Services under your leadership continues to politicize routine vaccinations, sowing doubt in patients and jeopardizing the health of our nation,” they continued.

The letter did not directly reference any anti-vaccine rhetoric from Urato, though he stated in a post on the social platform X last year: “The science is not ‘long-settled’ regarding vaccines.” He is also a known skeptic of women using antidepressants while pregnant.    

“We are deeply concerned that the addition of Dr. Urato and Dr. Biss to ACIP do not make pregnant women and their children healthier. Instead, their false, harmful ideologies put women and their children’s lives at risk,” the lawmakers wrote.

“The opinions of Dr. Urato and Dr. Biss are not founded in evidence-based science, but in anti-vaccine rhetoric that harms public health as a whole,” they continued.

The group also urged Kennedy to reinstate the 17 members he fired from ACIP last year, whom he replaced with a handpicked panel as part of an effort to overhaul U.S. vaccine policy.

“What was once a trusted panel of vaccine experts has become a hand-selected group of unqualified vaccine skeptics who have undermined years of scientific integrity and vetted public health practices,” the letter stated.

Reps. Suzan DelBene (Wash.), Kim Schrier (Wash.), Brad Schneider (Ill.) and Kelly Morrison (Minn.) led the letter, which 40 other House Democrats also signed.

data.title: Watch live: Minnesota, DHS officials testify before Senate on immigration enforcement
data.description:
The Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee on Thursday morning will hear testimony from the Trump administration and Minnesota officials on federal immigration enforcement efforts in the North Star State.

Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison (D) and Minnesota Department of Corrections Commissioner Paul Schnell will testify on the first panel. The second panel will include U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services Director Joseph Edlow, Customs and Border Protection (CBP) Commissioner Rodney Scott and Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) Acting Director Todd Lyons.

The hearings come two days after the DHS officials were grilled in the House and as ICE and CBP face scrutiny following the fatal shootings of two Minneapolis residents last month by federal agents. Lawmakers are also staring down a potential shutdown for the agencies after Friday as funding talks have fizzled.

The event is scheduled to begin at 9 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: White House makes last-minute bid to avoid DHS shutdown
data.description:
The White House made a last minute effort to avoid a shutdown of the Department of Homeland Security ahead of the funding deadline to keep the agency fully operational.

A White House official confirmed to The Hill that it sent legislative text of an offer to Democrats on Capitol Hill on Wednesday evening. 

However, Democrats are unlikely to accept the text, with leaders rejecting a proposal earlier this week from the White House as “incomplete and insufficient.” 

Senate Democrats said on Wednesday that negotiations with the White House have not made enough progress for them to real a deal on funding DHS, arguing they will not vote for another stopgap unless Republicans agree to a 10-point plan to overhaul Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Customs and Border Protection (CBP). 

Sen. Jacky Rosen (D-Nevada) said on Wednesday that the White House responded with a “one-pager” that did not include details after Democratic negotiators sent in detailed legislative text for reforming ICE last week. 

“That shows me that they’re not really serious about this,” she said before predicting Republicans will just keep offering short-term funding extensions to avoid having to agree to substantial immigration reforms.

“It’s like a kid who says, ‘Mom, just one more story, just one more story,’ and pretty soon they’re up all night,’” Rosen said.

data.title: Watch live: House Democrats give remarks on ICE accountability
data.description:
Top House Democrats and members of the Steering and Policy Committee will speak Thursday morning on holding Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) accountable as the battle for funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) persists.

Their remarks come as Democrats in both chambers have pushed their GOP colleagues to support reforms at DHS following two fatal shootings in Minneapolis last month at the hands of federal immigration authorities. Many in the party have also called on DHS Secretary Kristi Noem to resign or face impeachment proceedings over the department’s handling of President Trump’s mass deportation agenda.

ICE Acting Director Todd Lyons and other top DHS officials were grilled by lawmakers in the House earlier this week on the administration’s use of force during operations. Lawmakers now have until Friday evening to pass legislation to avoid a partial government shutdown that could affect more than just immigration.

The hearing will be led by House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.), Minority Whip Katherine Clark (D-Mass.) and Democratic Caucus Chair Pete Aguilar (D-Calif.).

The event is scheduled to begin at 9 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Raskin accuses Bondi, DOJ of 'Orwellian' surveillance
data.description:
Rep. Jamie Raskin (D-Md.) on Wednesday sharply criticized Attorney General Pam Bondi and the Department of Justice (DOJ) for seemingly surveilling lawmakers’ search histories of the Jeffrey Epstein files.

During Bondi’s testimony Wednesday before the House Judiciary Committee, Getty photographer Roberto Schmidt captured a list of items that Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.) searched in the files. Earlier this week, members of Congress were given access to the unredacted Epstein files at DOJ headquarters.

“It is a violation of the separation of powers for them to essentially be monitoring our computer searches on the Epstein files,” Raskin later told reporters on the Capitol steps, according to journalist Pablo Manriquez.

“So, I think it’s outrageous that they would do that, and it’s Orwellian and we’re going to have some more to say about it soon,” the lawmaker added.

Raskin, ranking member on the Judiciary Committee, added that “we have reason to believe that it was happening to everybody that went over there, that they were monitoring everybody’s computer searches.”

When pressed on what gives him that notion, the Maryland Democrat said, “We’ll be having more to say about this later.”

Throughout her testimony, Bondi got into intense shouting matches with multiple Democratic lawmakers, along with Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.). Lawmakers have criticized the DOJ for its handling of the release of files related to the convicted sex offender, particularly the redactions of certain names and the failure to conceal the names of multiple victims.

At least 11 victims of Epstein were in the audience — with some wearing shirts referencing the redactions. When Jayapal asked the victims to raise their hands if they had not yet met with the DOJ, they each lifted a hand.

Regarding the DOJ tracking her search history, Jayapal said Wednesday on MS NOW that it amounts to “spying” and she plans on speaking to House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) on her concerns.

“Was she going to use that against me? Did they get that document for every single person? And what were they going to do with it? We need a new process,” she told the outlet.

data.title: Watch live: Homan gives remarks from Minnesota amid ICE scrutiny
data.description:
Border czar Tom Homan will speak with reporters Thursday morning from Minneapolis on the Trump administration’s immigration operation in the city — which is still reeling from two fatal shootings by federal agents last month.

President Trump sent Homan to oversee “Operation Metro Surge” after protests spread across the state following the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti. In wake of scrutiny over Immigration and Customs Enforcement’s (ICE) use of force, the border czar earlier this month said the administration would pull back some federal authorities from the city.

The president has signaled that his administration could take a “softer” approach to immigration enforcement and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) said earlier this week that he expects the crackdown in the North Star State to end soon.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 9 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Without drastic changes, Republicans' 2026 hopes are on ice
data.description:
Republican prospects in the upcoming midterm election took a serious hit in the frigid January air of Minnesota. The damage to Republicans was mostly self inflicted.

It engulfed Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, who was so eager to get in front of the cameras after immigration enforcement officers fatally shot two American citizens that she forgot to bring anything with her resembling truth or accuracy. It then snared Border Patrol Commander Greg Bovino, who insisted that officers involved in the scuffle that preceded the second shooting were actually the victims. 

Six hours later, President Trump, exhibiting more political sense than some of the people around him, ordered Bovino out of Minneapolis to a new assignment in San Francisco. He sent Border Czar Tom Homan to Minneapolis in an effort to cool things down. Homan’s subsequent withdrawal of 700 federal officers from Minneapolis is a start.

Minnesota, as you may recall, is the land of multi-billion dollar food-aid and child-care fraud. Americans, and importantly the media, do not find the investigative work necessary to find fraud nearly as exciting as the 3,000 ICE officers on city streets pulling American citizens out of their vehicles.

The fraud issues were made-to-order for a Republican Party that has spent decades trying to reduce spending and tighten regulations on similar social service programs. And yet, on immigration enforcement, Republicans in Washington overreacted, exaggerated and forgot the middle of the political spectrum.

Noem accused one victim of “domestic terrorism.” White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller called the second victim a “would-be assassin.”

The argument from defenders of the immigration enforcement officials’ actions goes against a constitutional principle the Republican Party has embraced for decades. Let’s stipulate that it was dumb of Alex Pretti to carry his loaded handgun to a protest against ICE — but also that dumb is not necessarily illegal. Any federal law enforcement officer who does not understand that the Second Amendment and Minnesota state law gives any citizen with a permit the right to carry a weapon openly, as Pretti did, has no place on the streets of America. 

It is up to the people in charge, as well as Congress, to ensure that these officers, carrying live ammunition, get proper training before they are allowed to interact with American citizens.

Republicans attempting to justify Alex Pretti’s shooting because he had a weapon risk splitting their coalition from one of its traditionally solid planks. For decades, the Republican Party, in conjunction with the National Rifle Association, has spent millions of dollars to implement the very law under which Pretti was carrying his weapon. 

Every Republican consultant knows that getting an A-rating from the NRA for your candidate is extremely important, especially in the primary. For Trump-appointed officials to suggest that carrying a weapon within the confines of existing law is justification for law enforcement to use lethal force is beyond political stupidity. It is abhorrent in every way.

The White House goal of arresting 3,000 illegal immigrants per day is unattainable by arresting only “the worst of worst.” The talking points being used by Republicans have lost credibility. The numbers simply do not work, as the editorial board of the Wall Street Journal recently demonstrated.

Immigration enforcement needs a total reset. It should be run by people with significant law enforcement experience, not by politically connected figures big on campaign promises and short on law enforcement experience.

Voters’ support for ICE tactics is dwindling by the day. Without significant reform, it will soon be a very heavy burden for Republican candidates across the board in November.

The Jan. 31 special election in Ft. Worth for a seat in the Texas state Senate is likely a preview of Republican prospects for November. The Republican candidate outspent her Democratic opponent 10 to 1 and still lost by 14 points in a district Trump had carried 15 months ago by 17 points. Republican activists are going to be shocked if and when they finally realize that Trump administration policies are bringing the Senate into play for a possible Democratic takeover.

The Fox News January poll shows that 59 percent think ICE tactics have been too aggressive. That 59 percent includes 30 percent of Republican women, 49 percent of non-MAGA Republicans, 71 percent of independents and even 26 percent of Trump voters.

Republican leadership in Washington, from the Republican National Committee to Capitol Hill, and especially to the White House, have been very slow to recognize that Republicans have a significant midterm problem. Since last April, in election after election, Republicans have substantially underperformed their previous showing. Stubbornness and egocentric policies emanating from the White House are a prescription for a massive defeat.

As one Republican member of the House recently said “Many of us wonder if the administration has any clue as to how much this will hurt us legislatively and electorally this year.” Apparently not, representative.

Kevin Igoe is the former deputy chief of staff of the Republican National Committee and former executive director of Maryland Republican Party. He served as chief of staff of the Maryland Department of Budget and Management and was a Reagan White House appointee.
